content.problemStatement:
Tweedle-Dee and Tweedle-Dum are in a fierce battle playing even-odd nim. This novel game is played on **N** heaps. Heap **i** contains **ai** stones.


Like normal nim, Tweedle-Dee and Tweedle-Dum alternate taking a positive number of stones from any single one of the heaps, and the player that can't remove stones loses. However Tweedle-Dee can only take an even number of stones, and Tweedle-Dum can only take an odd number of stones.

Alice doesn't want to wait until the end of the game, so she asks you to determine the winner of the game. Remember that Tweedle-Dee and Tweedle-Dum are legendary grandmasters of combinatorial games, so they always play optimally.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains an integer N, the number of heaps, and a string P, the player that starts the game. The second line contains N space-separated integers, the number of stones in each heap.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing either 'Dee' if Tweedle-Dee wins the game or 'Dum' otherwise.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Tweedle-Dee takes two stones from any of the heaps leaving just one non-empty heap, next Tweedle-Dum takes one stone and the game finishes.
Tweedle-Dum takes one stone, then Tweedle-Dee takes two stones, leaving no stones for Tweedle-Dum to take.
Tweedle-Dum takes one stone from the first heap, then Tweedle-Dee takes two stones from the second heap, leaving no stones for Tweedle-Dum to take.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use the concept of nim-sum. However, since Tweedle-Dee can only take an even number of stones and Tweedle-Dum can only take an odd number of stones, we need to consider the parity of the number of stones in each heap. We can calculate the nim-sum of the parities of the number of stones in each heap. If the nim-sum is not zero, then the first player has a winning strategy. Otherwise, the second player has a winning strategy.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T*N) — where T is the number of test cases and N is the number of heaps in each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — since we only need a constant amount of space to store the nim-sum and the result.
